source: Invested by Aleph — Semiconductors, China/Taiwan fabs, global warming (China's Not Getting Taiwan's Fabs)
summary: Israeli venture semis and cyber are attractive because founders are experienced operators (age ~50) with proven track records, unlike typical software startups
summary_extended: Baker argues that semiconductor venture investing offers a structurally superior risk-adjusted return profile compared to software venture because the founders being backed are typically experienced operators around age 50 who previously ran divisions at major semiconductor companies and bring their entire teams with them. This dramatically reduces execution risk relative to backing first-time founders in their mid-to-late twenties, as is common in software startups. Israeli cybersecurity venture shares this same characteristic of experienced, proven founders, making it similarly attractive. Atreides has concentrated approximately 30-40% of its venture portfolio in Israel to capitalize on this dynamic. The investment implication is a deliberate overweight to Israeli semiconductor and cyber venture deals as a risk-adjusted alpha source, though no specific publicly listed equities are the subject of this thesis.
detail: Baker argues semiconductor venture investing is fundamentally different from software venture because you're often backing a 50-year-old who ran a division at a major company and brought their team. Israeli cyber shares this characteristic. This reduces execution risk vs. backing 25-30 year old first-time founders. Atreides has ~30-40% of its venture portfolio in Israel.
confidence_evidence: Baker explicitly calls this out as a capital deployment priority and explains the structural reason for the risk-adjusted advantage
quote_fragment: in semiconductors often you're investing in a 50-year-old who ran a division
